title: Confusion-Calibrated Supervised Contrastive Learning: Adaptive Class-Pair Reweighting from Training Dynamics
description: We propose CC-SupCon, a method that dynamically modulates the repulsion strength between class pairs in supervised contrastive learning based on a running confusability matrix computed from the model's own representation geometry. Unlike standard SupCon which treats all negative pairs equally (converging to a simplex ETF under neural collapse), CC-SupCon increases repulsion for class pairs that the model currently confuses and relaxes it for well-separated pairs, creating an implicit coarse-to-fine curriculum.
motivation: Standard supervised contrastive learning treats all negative class pairs equally, leading to representations that converge to an equidistant simplex ETF geometry (neural collapse). This wastes representational capacity on already well-separated classes while under-investing in separating confusable classes. While prior work has explored confusion-guided contrastive learning—Lovász Theta (Smyrnis et al., 2022) softens repulsion for semantically similar classes and CCDC (Chen et al., 2024) uses confusion degree for hard class mining in segmentation—no existing method dynamically upweights repulsion for confusable class pairs in image-level supervised contrastive learning using a momentum-updated confusability matrix derived from the model's own training dynamics.
proposed_approach: CC-SupCon maintains exponential moving average class centroids and computes a C x C confusability matrix measuring pairwise class proximity in the current representation space. This matrix modulates negative pair weights in the SupCon loss: confusable class pairs receive higher weights (stronger repulsion) while well-separated pairs receive baseline weights. The confusability matrix is updated each batch with momentum, creating a self-calibrating system where the loss automatically focuses on the hardest class distinctions as training progresses. Unlike Lovász Theta which decreases repulsion for similar classes (preserving semantic similarity), CC-SupCon increases repulsion for confused classes (resolving discrimination failures). Unlike CCDC which uses binary hard class mining for segmentation, CC-SupCon uses continuous soft weights for image classification. Class-pair-level weighting provides lower gradient variance than instance-level hard negative mining (Robinson et al., 2021; Jiang et al., 2022) because it averages over all pairs within a class combination.

related_work.title: Supervised Contrastive Learning
related_work.relationship: Foundation that CC-SupCon extends. SupCon treats all negative pairs equally; we add confusability-based weighting.

related_work.title: Lovász Theta Contrastive Learning
related_work.relationship: Closest prior work. Uses a learned weighted similarity graph to soften (decrease) repulsion for similar classes. CC-SupCon takes the opposite direction: increasing repulsion for confusable classes. The distinction is between preserving semantic similarity (Lovász Theta) and resolving model confusion (CC-SupCon). Additionally, Lovász Theta uses a fixed/learned graph while CC-SupCon's matrix evolves dynamically with training.

related_work.title: Dynamic Contrastive Learning Guided by Class Confidence and Confusion Degree for Medical Image Segmentation
related_work.authors: Chen, Chen, Huang, Zhang, Debattista, Han
related_work.relationship: Uses inter-class confusion for hard class mining in pixel-level contrastive learning for segmentation. CC-SupCon differs: (i) image-level not pixel-level, (ii) continuous soft weights not binary mining, (iii) momentum-updated confusability matrix for stable curriculum.

related_work.title: Prevalence of Neural Collapse during the terminal phase of deep learning training
related_work.relationship: Shows that supervised training converges to simplex ETF (all classes equidistant). CC-SupCon intentionally breaks this symmetry for better representations.

related_work.title: Dissecting Supervised Contrastive Learning
related_work.relationship: Proves SupCon loss also minimizes at simplex ETF. Provides theoretical grounding for why equal treatment of negative pairs leads to equidistant geometry.

related_work.title: Contrastive Learning with Hard Negative Samples
related_work.relationship: Instance-level hard negative mining in contrastive learning. CC-SupCon operates at the class-pair level, which provides lower gradient variance (see variance reduction argument in proposal).

related_work.title: Supervised Contrastive Learning with Hard Negative Samples
related_work.relationship: Extends hard negative mining to the supervised setting. Used as a baseline (H-SCL) to compare instance-level vs. class-pair-level approaches.

related_work.title: Balanced Contrastive Learning for Long-Tailed Visual Recognition
related_work.relationship: Addresses class imbalance in SupCon via class-averaging. Orthogonal to CC-SupCon which addresses class confusability (difficulty of separating classes), not frequency imbalance.

related_work.title: Understanding Contrastive Representation Learning through Alignment and Uniformity on the Hypersphere
related_work.relationship: Identifies alignment and uniformity as key representation quality metrics. CC-SupCon adds a third consideration: calibrated inter-class separation.

related_work.title: Variational Supervised Contrastive Learning
related_work.relationship: VarCon uses confidence-adaptive temperature per sample via variational inference. CC-SupCon adapts weights per class-pair based on confusability. The two approaches are complementary.

related_work.title: Use All the Labels: A Hierarchical Multi-Label Contrastive Learning Framework
related_work.relationship: Uses explicit label hierarchies to structure contrastive learning. CC-SupCon discovers inter-class structure from training dynamics without external annotations.

hypothesis: Adaptively reweighting negative pairs in supervised contrastive learning based on class-pair confusability scores derived from the model's evolving representation geometry will produce more discriminative representations (higher linear probe accuracy) and more structured representations (better transfer and few-shot performance) compared to standard SupCon, without requiring external knowledge such as label hierarchies.

primary: CC-SupCon achieves statistically significantly higher linear probe accuracy than standard SupCon on CIFAR-100 (paired t-test, p < 0.05, 5 seeds with same seed pairing)
The learned confusability matrix shows meaningful structure (higher within-superclass confusability than between-superclass on CIFAR-100)
secondary: Improvements on k-NN evaluation and transfer learning tasks
Ablation studies confirm contribution of confusability weighting (beta > 0 improves over beta = 0)
CC-SupCon is less sensitive to batch size than standard SupCon (batch size sensitivity experiment at sizes 128, 256, 512, 1024)
Gradient analysis confirms larger gradient magnitudes for confusable pairs under CC-SupCon and lower gradient variance than instance-level H-SCL
failure_indicators: CC-SupCon performs worse than or identical to SupCon on all metrics
Confusability matrix shows no meaningful structure (random noise)
Results are highly sensitive to beta with no robust operating range

feasibility.compute: ~7.2 hours on 1x NVIDIA RTX A6000 (48GB VRAM). Focused on CIFAR-100 with 5 seeds for main comparison, 3 seeds for ablations.
feasibility.datasets: CIFAR-100 (freely available, 20-superclass structure ideal for confusability analysis)
feasibility.implementation_complexity: Low — only requires maintaining class centroids (O(Cd) per batch) and computing a C x C confusability matrix (O(C^2 d)). Can be implemented as a drop-in replacement for the SupCon loss.
feasibility.risk_factors: The confusability signal may be noisy early in training; mitigated by high momentum (alpha=0.99) and optional warmup period. Lovász Theta and CCDC address related ideas but in different directions/domains, confirming the space is active but our specific approach is novel.
